puzzles.title: The Obama Wave
puzzles.intro: Barack Obama arrives with a filibuster-proof majority, a financial crisis on every desk, and an agenda the size of the New Deal. Every big vote comes down to a handful of independent minds.

senators.name: Susan Collins
senators.blurb: A Maine moderate and one of the last New England Republicans. She prides herself on bipartisan deal-making — she helped write down the 2009 stimulus — but guards her independence on social issues.

senators.name: Ben Nelson
senators.blurb: Perhaps the most conservative Democrat in the chamber, from deep-red Nebraska. He haggles for home-state concessions and agonizes publicly before every big vote.

senators.name: Bernie Sanders
senators.blurb: The Senate's democratic socialist, caucusing with Democrats under his own flag. He wants Medicare-for-all and blames Wall Street for most of America's ills — the question is whether half-measures earn his vote.

senators.name: John McCain
senators.blurb: Fresh off losing the presidency to Obama, the old maverick has turned partisan brawler, opposing most of the new administration's agenda while keeping his hawkish, reform-minded reputation.

senators.name: Russ Feingold
senators.blurb: A civil-libertarian progressive from Wisconsin who treats principle as non-negotiable. He'll vote against his own party when a bill doesn't go far enough, and he's famous for casting lonely no votes.

bills.name: The Affordable Care Act
bills.desc: December 24, 2009, 7 a.m. — the Senate's first Christmas Eve vote in 114 years. Obama's health-care overhaul: an individual mandate to buy insurance, subsidized exchanges, a Medicaid expansion, and a ban on denying coverage for pre-existing conditions. After a year of town-hall fury, Democrats need all 60 caucus votes to break the filibuster.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 3590

bills.name: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform
bills.desc: July 2010. The answer to the financial crisis: a new consumer-protection bureau, derivatives regulation, and authority to wind down 'too big to fail' banks. Wall Street hates it — and some progressives say it's still far too soft on the megabanks.
bills.question: On the Conference Report H.R. 4173

bills.name: Repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell
bills.desc: December 2010, the lame-duck session. A standalone bill to end the 17-year ban on gay and lesbian troops serving openly, after a Pentagon study found repeal would not harm military readiness.
bills.question: On the Motion (Motion to Concur in the House Amendment to the Senate Amendment to H.R. 2965)

puzzles.title: After September 11
puzzles.intro: Anthrax in the mailroom, troops mobilizing, flags on every lapel. A Senate under pressure to move fast — on surveillance, on schools, and finally on war.

senators.name: Russ Feingold
senators.blurb: Wisconsin's civil-libertarian conscience — deeply skeptical of government surveillance and of blank-check war powers, even when the whole country is demanding action.

senators.name: Hillary Clinton
senators.blurb: The former First Lady, ten months into her Senate career, representing the state that was attacked. She is building a hawkish, centrist national-security profile — with an eye, everyone assumes, on bigger things.

senators.name: Lincoln Chafee
senators.blurb: The Senate's most liberal Republican, an heir to Yankee moderation. He regularly opposes his own party on taxes, the environment, and the use of force.

senators.name: Robert Byrd
senators.blurb: The Senate's octogenarian institutionalist, a copy of the Constitution in his breast pocket. He defends Congress's war powers against any president, of either party.

senators.name: Ted Kennedy
senators.blurb: The liberal lion. A master legislator happy to partner with George W. Bush on domestic causes he cares about — education above all — while fighting him on nearly everything else.

bills.name: The USA PATRIOT Act
bills.desc: October 25, 2001 — six weeks after the attacks. Sweeping new surveillance powers: roving wiretaps, expanded FISA authority, 'sneak and peek' searches, and government access to business records. It is moving so fast that most senators admit they haven't read it.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 3162

bills.name: No Child Left Behind
bills.desc: December 2001. Bush's signature education law: annual testing, public school report cards, and consequences for failing schools — negotiated hand-in-hand with leading Democrats and paired with a big increase in federal education spending.
bills.question: On the Conference Report H.R. 1

bills.name: Authorization for War in Iraq
bills.desc: October 11, 2002, three weeks before the midterms. The resolution authorizes the President to use the Armed Forces against Saddam Hussein's Iraq at a time of his choosing. Intelligence about weapons of mass destruction is everywhere. So are doubts.
bills.question: On the Joint Resolution H.J.Res. 114

puzzles.title: The Class of 2008
puzzles.intro: Five senators — four of whom want the same promotion. Watch how a presidential campaign, and then a financial collapse, shapes a voting record.

senators.name: Barack Obama
senators.blurb: The freshman phenomenon, running on his early opposition to the Iraq war. Cautious and consensus-minded on most everything else — he can't afford a vote that reads as radical.

senators.name: Hillary Clinton
senators.blurb: The frontrunner, defending the hawkish national-security record she built after 9/11 while a primary electorate pulls her left.

senators.name: John McCain
senators.blurb: The Republican nominee-in-waiting: a national-security hawk and deficit scold with a history of party apostasy he's currently trying to live down.

senators.name: Joe Biden
senators.blurb: Chairman of Foreign Relations, blue-collar Democrat from Delaware, long-shot candidate running on judgment and experience.

senators.name: Bernie Sanders
senators.blurb: The freshman socialist, not running for anything — yet. Economics first, and no patience for rescuing the people he blames for the mess.

bills.name: Minimum Wage Increase
bills.desc: February 2007. The new Democratic majority's first order of business: raising the federal minimum wage from $5.15 to $7.25 over two years, sweetened with small-business tax breaks to bring Republicans along.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 2

bills.name: Iraq War Funding — No Timeline
bills.desc: May 24, 2007. Bush has just vetoed a war-funding bill because it included a withdrawal timeline. This is the replacement: $95 billion for the troops with the timeline stripped out. Anti-war groups call voting yes a capitulation; opponents of the bill are accused of defunding troops in the field.
bills.question: On the Motion (Motion to Concur in House Amdt. to Senate Amdt to H.R.2206)

bills.name: The $700B Bank Bailout (TARP)
bills.desc: October 1, 2008. Lehman is dead, credit is frozen, and the Treasury Secretary wants $700 billion to rescue the banking system. Both presidential nominees fly back to Washington for the vote. Main Street is furious.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 1424

puzzles.title: Guns & Borders
puzzles.intro: After Sandy Hook, and after an election Republicans lost badly with Hispanic voters, the Senate attempts guns and immigration in one year — then shuts the government down arguing about health care.

senators.name: Joe Manchin
senators.blurb: A gun-owning, coal-country Democrat from a state Obama just lost by 27 points. He holds an NRA 'A' rating and protects his centrist brand fiercely — but Sandy Hook shook him.

senators.name: Pat Toomey
senators.blurb: A former Club for Growth president — about as fiscally conservative as they come — now representing purple Pennsylvania, which expects the occasional pragmatic turn.

senators.name: Ted Cruz
senators.blurb: Three months into his first term and already the Senate's chief insurgent. He came to Washington to fight the establishments of both parties, not to make deals.

senators.name: Elizabeth Warren
senators.blurb: The consumer-protection professor turned senator — the new standard-bearer of the party's progressive wing.

senators.name: Mark Kirk
senators.blurb: A moderate, socially liberal Republican from deep-blue Illinois, recently back from a stroke, facing voters who just backed Obama twice.

bills.name: Manchin-Toomey Background Checks
bills.desc: April 17, 2013 — four months after Sandy Hook. A bipartisan compromise extending background checks to gun shows and internet sales, far short of an assault-weapons ban, polling near 90% support. Under the deal in force, it needs 60 votes.
bills.question: On the Amendment S.Amdt. 715 to S. 649 (Safe Communities, Safe Schools Act of2013)

bills.name: The Gang of Eight Immigration Bill
bills.desc: June 2013. Comprehensive immigration reform: a 13-year path to citizenship for 11 million undocumented immigrants, a doubling of the Border Patrol, 700 miles of fencing, and mandatory E-Verify.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill S. 744

bills.name: Reopening the Government
bills.desc: October 16, 2013 — day 16 of the shutdown, hours from a debt default. The deal reopens the government and raises the debt ceiling with essentially nothing given to the defund-Obamacare campaign that started it.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 2775

puzzles.title: Three Votes to Lose
puzzles.intro: Unified Republican government and a razor-thin Senate. With almost no margin for error, every landmark vote hangs on a handful of senators who answer to no one.

senators.name: Susan Collins
senators.blurb: Twenty years in, the chamber's definitive swing vote: pro-choice, deficit-conscious, protective of Maine's rural hospitals, and increasingly willing to buck her party's new president.

senators.name: Lisa Murkowski
senators.blurb: Alaska's senator owes her seat to a write-in campaign, not the party — she once lost her own primary and won anyway. Energy development and Alaska's native healthcare systems outrank party loyalty.

senators.name: Joe Manchin
senators.blurb: Still the reddest-state Democrat, now facing re-election in a state Trump carried by 42 points. He votes with the President more than any other Democrat — when he thinks West Virginia wants it.

senators.name: Rand Paul
senators.blurb: The libertarian: wants government out of health care, taxes near zero, and deficits actually shrinking. Happily votes against Republican bills for being insufficiently conservative.

senators.name: Heidi Heitkamp
senators.blurb: A North Dakota Democrat holding the Senate's most endangered seat, balancing her party's base against a state Trump won by 36. Every vote is a re-election calculation.

bills.name: 'Skinny' Obamacare Repeal
bills.desc: July 28, 2017, 1:30 a.m. After full repeal has already failed, Republicans try 'skinny repeal' — killing the individual mandate and a few taxes — as a vehicle to keep the repeal effort alive in conference. Leadership swears it will never become law as written. Fifty votes will do it, and the floor is thick with rumors.
bills.question: On the Amendment S.Amdt. 667 to S.Amdt. 267 to H.R. 1628 (American Health Care Act of 2017)

bills.name: The Trump Tax Cuts
bills.desc: December 2, 2017, nearly 2 a.m. The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act: the corporate rate slashed from 35% to 20%, individual cuts that expire after 2025, the ACA's mandate penalty zeroed out, and roughly $1.4 trillion added to the deficit. Handwritten amendments are still being scribbled in the margins.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 1

bills.name: Confirming Brett Kavanaugh
bills.desc: October 6, 2018. After Christine Blasey Ford's testimony and a supplemental FBI investigation, the Supreme Court confirmation comes to the floor with the chamber — and the country — at a boil. A simple majority confirms.
bills.question: On the Nomination PN2259

notes.murkowski: Murkowski's declared vote was No. She withdrew it and voted 'present' as a courtesy 'live pair' with Steve Daines (R-MT), a Kavanaugh supporter away at his daughter's wedding — old Senate custom, same effect as a Nay.

puzzles.title: November 1993
puzzles.intro: A young President Clinton, a Democratic Congress full of Southern conservatives, and one extraordinary week in November: trade, guns, and crime, back to back.

senators.name: Joe Biden
senators.blurb: Two decades in, chairman of the Judiciary Committee: a deal-making Delaware Democrat, tough-on-crime by design and trade-friendly by instinct.

senators.name: Robert Byrd
senators.blurb: The Appropriations king and guardian of West Virginia's mines and mills. Suspicious of anything that might ship jobs overseas; devoted to the working man as he defines him.

senators.name: John McCain
senators.blurb: An Arizona conservative with a westerner's view of government: pro-trade, pro-military, and as skeptical of gun laws as his voters are.

senators.name: Dianne Feinstein
senators.blurb: San Francisco's former mayor, in the Senate less than a year. She became mayor the day a colleague was assassinated at City Hall — gun violence is personal, and she has chosen it as her first great fight.

senators.name: Jesse Helms
senators.blurb: 'Senator No.' The hard-right anchor of the chamber: anti-communist, protectionist where tobacco and textiles are concerned, and against nearly anything with 'new' in the name.

bills.name: The Assault Weapons Ban
bills.desc: November 17, 1993. An amendment to the crime bill banning the manufacture of 19 named semiautomatic 'assault weapons' and high-capacity magazines for ten years. Even many Democrats from rural states are queasy.
bills.question: On the Amendment S.Amdt. 1152 to S.Amdt. 1151 to S. 1607 (Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act of 1993)

bills.name: The Brady Bill
bills.desc: November 20, 1993. A five-day waiting period and background check for handgun purchases, named for Reagan's press secretary, shot and paralyzed in 1981. The NRA has fought it for seven years straight.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 1025

bills.name: NAFTA
bills.desc: Also November 20, 1993. The North American Free Trade Agreement — negotiated by Bush, embraced by Clinton over his own party's objections and organized labor's fury. Ross Perot says you can already hear a 'giant sucking sound' of jobs going south.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 3450

puzzles.title: Line in the Sand
puzzles.intro: Half a million American troops sit in the Saudi desert while Congress holds its first real war debate since Vietnam. Nine months later, a Supreme Court nomination ends in the most explosive hearings ever held.

senators.name: Al Gore
senators.blurb: A cerebral Tennessee moderate with presidential ambitions and a hawkish streak rare in his party's rising generation. He agonizes over big votes — and enjoys telling you so.

senators.name: Sam Nunn
senators.blurb: Chairman of Armed Services and the Democrats' most trusted voice on the military. A Georgia conservative — but a believer that sanctions and patience can win wars cheaper than blood.

senators.name: Bob Dole
senators.blurb: The Republican leader: a wounded World War II veteran, sharp-tongued dealmaker, and reliable soldier for the Bush White House.

senators.name: John Kerry
senators.blurb: A decorated Vietnam veteran who came home to protest the war. Two decades later, deeply wary of committing American troops before every alternative is exhausted.

senators.name: Nancy Kassebaum
senators.blurb: A plainspoken Kansas internationalist, budget-minded and independent — one of only two women in the chamber.

bills.name: Authorizing the Gulf War
bills.desc: January 12, 1991. Saddam Hussein has occupied Kuwait for five months, and the UN deadline expires in three days. The resolution authorizes the President to drive Iraq out by force. The debate is somber and the outcome genuinely in doubt — the closest vote to authorize war in American history.
bills.question: On the Joint Resolution S.J.Res. 2

bills.name: Confirming Clarence Thomas
bills.desc: October 15, 1991. Clarence Thomas's Supreme Court nomination reaches the floor days after Anita Hill's testimony transfixed the country. He would fill Thurgood Marshall's seat — and shift the Court for a generation.
bills.question: On the Nomination PN456

bills.name: The Civil Rights Act of 1991
bills.desc: October 30, 1991. After two years of veto fights over 'quotas,' a compromise civil-rights bill restoring workers' power to sue for discrimination — finally pre-cleared with the White House.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill S. 1745

puzzles.title: The Republican Revolution
puzzles.intro: Two years after Gingrich's landslide, a Democratic president runs toward the center with an election looming. Welfare, marriage, wages — 1996 is one long argument about what the parties are for.

senators.name: Daniel Patrick Moynihan
senators.blurb: The Senate's scholar — a Harvard sociologist who wrote the book on poverty policy, literally. Party loyalty means little when he believes a bill will hurt the poor.

senators.name: Joe Lieberman
senators.blurb: A moralist Connecticut Democrat comfortable scolding his own party: pro-business, hawkish, and outspoken about family values.

senators.name: Barbara Boxer
senators.blurb: A Marin County liberal and one of the chamber's most reliable progressive votes, especially where civil rights are concerned.

senators.name: Rick Santorum
senators.blurb: The youngest Republican senator: a combative Pennsylvania culture warrior who courts blue-collar Catholics with the occasional populist economic vote.

senators.name: Phil Gramm
senators.blurb: A Texas economics professor turned budget hawk, fresh off a presidential run about slashing government. If a bill spends money or regulates business, count him out.

bills.name: Welfare Reform
bills.desc: August 1, 1996. 'The end of welfare as we know it': time limits, work requirements, and block grants replacing a six-decade federal guarantee for poor families. Clinton has vetoed two versions and promised to sign this one. Half his party feels betrayed.
bills.question: On the Conference Report H.R. 3734

bills.name: The Defense of Marriage Act
bills.desc: September 10, 1996. DOMA: no federal recognition of same-sex marriages, and no state required to honor another state's. Clinton says he'll sign it. Only a handful of senators are willing to be counted against it.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 3396

bills.name: Minimum Wage Increase
bills.desc: July 9, 1996. A 90-cent raise in the minimum wage — $4.25 to $5.15 — riding on a package of small-business tax breaks. Election-year pressure has cracked the Republican leadership's resistance.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 3448

puzzles.title: America & the World
puzzles.intro: With the Cold War won, Washington argues about what comes next: disarmament treaties, an expanding NATO, and whether to bet the future on trade with China.

senators.name: Jesse Helms
senators.blurb: Chairman of Foreign Relations and scourge of the striped-pants set. Treaties, foreign aid, the UN — he distrusts them all, and buries what he distrusts.

senators.name: Richard Lugar
senators.blurb: The Republicans' soft-spoken foreign-policy intellectual, co-author of the program dismantling loose Soviet nukes. An internationalist to his fingertips.

senators.name: Daniel Patrick Moynihan
senators.blurb: The Senate's contrarian scholar — a former UN ambassador with his own theories about secrecy, ethnicity, and international law, cheerfully immune to conventional wisdom.

senators.name: Paul Wellstone
senators.blurb: The happiest warrior of the left: a former wrestling coach and campus organizer who votes his conscience on labor and human rights, popularity be damned.

senators.name: Joe Biden
senators.blurb: The Democrats' garrulous front man on Foreign Relations — an institutionalist who believes in American alliances and American engagement, almost anywhere.

bills.name: The Chemical Weapons Treaty
bills.desc: April 24, 1997. The Chemical Weapons Convention: a global ban on producing and stockpiling chemical arms, signed by the first President Bush. Ratification needs 67 votes, and conservatives warn it is unverifiable and toothless.
bills.question: On the Resolution of Ratification S.Res. 75

bills.name: Expanding NATO Eastward
bills.desc: April 30, 1998. Admitting Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic to NATO — extending America's defense umbrella to Russia's old doorstep. Critics ask what the alliance is now for, and whom it might provoke. Treaties need 67 votes.
bills.question: On the Resolution of Ratification Treaty Doc. 105-36

bills.name: Permanent Trade with China
bills.desc: September 19, 2000. Permanent normal trade relations with China, clearing its path into the World Trade Organization. Business is euphoric. Labor and human-rights advocates call it a historic sellout.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 4444

puzzles.title: The Bush Majority
puzzles.intro: War in Iraq, tax cuts at home, and a Republican Party at high tide. For Democrats, every vote is a choice between conviction and survival.

senators.name: John McCain
senators.blurb: The maverick restored: hawkish on the war, but a deficit scold who has rediscovered his taste for bucking his own White House on spending.

senators.name: Ted Kennedy
senators.blurb: Forty years in and pugnacious as ever — he calls the Iraq war a 'fraud cooked up in Texas,' while still cutting domestic deals with this White House when it serves his causes.

senators.name: Tom Daschle
senators.blurb: The Democratic leader: a soft-spoken South Dakotan balancing a liberal caucus against a state Bush carried by 22 points — with his own re-election looming.

senators.name: Arlen Specter
senators.blurb: Pennsylvania's cranky Republican moderate: pro-choice, pro-labor by his party's standards, and facing a bruising primary from his right next spring.

senators.name: Zell Miller
senators.blurb: A Georgia Democrat in name only by now: he keynoted for Clinton in '92, but has embraced Bush's agenda nearly wholesale — and relishes infuriating his own party.

bills.name: The Partial-Birth Abortion Ban
bills.desc: March 13, 2003. A federal ban on the late-term procedure opponents call partial-birth abortion — the first federal criminal prohibition of an abortion method since Roe, with an exception for the mother's life but not her health.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill S. 3

bills.name: $87 Billion for Iraq
bills.desc: October 17, 2003. Eighty-seven billion dollars for the occupations of Iraq and Afghanistan — six months after 'Mission Accomplished,' with no weapons found and the insurgency growing. Voting no reads as abandoning the troops; voting yes as endorsing the war.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill S. 1689

bills.name: Medicare Prescription Drugs
bills.desc: November 25, 2003. The biggest Medicare expansion in history: a prescription-drug benefit delivered through private insurers, with the government barred from negotiating prices. Liberals call it a giveaway to industry; conservatives call it a budget-buster.
bills.question: On the Conference Report H.R. 1

puzzles.title: Second-Term Blues
puzzles.intro: A re-elected George W. Bush spends his political capital fast: bankruptcy law for the banks, a fence for the border, and military tribunals for the age of terror.

senators.name: Barack Obama
senators.blurb: The celebrity freshman, two years from announcing for president: a constitutional-law lecturer's instincts inside a cautious legislator's record.

senators.name: Joe Biden
senators.blurb: Foreign Relations' top Democrat — and Delaware's senator, which means the credit-card industry's senator, as his critics never tire of noting.

senators.name: Lincoln Chafee
senators.blurb: The chamber's loneliest Republican: anti-war, pro-environment, pro-civil-liberties, and fighting for his political life in deep-blue Rhode Island this November.

senators.name: Tom Coburn
senators.blurb: 'Dr. No.' An Oklahoma obstetrician who hunts wasteful spending for sport and votes against nearly anything that grows the government.

senators.name: Dianne Feinstein
senators.blurb: California's businesslike centrist: tough on crime and terrorism by her party's standards, a dealmaker on the Judiciary Committee.

bills.name: The Bankruptcy Bill
bills.desc: March 10, 2005. The credit-card industry's decade-long dream: means tests and new hurdles making it far harder for consumers to erase debts in bankruptcy. Banks say it targets abuse; consumer advocates say it punishes the sick, the divorced, and the broke.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill S. 256

bills.name: The Secure Fence Act
bills.desc: September 29, 2006. Seven hundred miles of double-layer fencing along the Mexican border, voted weeks before the midterms — the enforcement-only answer after comprehensive immigration reform collapsed.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 6061

bills.name: The Military Commissions Act
bills.desc: September 28, 2006. Military tribunals for Guantánamo detainees, stripped of habeas corpus review, with the CIA's interrogation program written into law — rushed through in the September dash to adjourn and campaign.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill S. 3930

puzzles.title: The Tea Party Senate
puzzles.intro: A debt-ceiling standoff brings the country within days of default, and the parties' new wings — Tea Party right, progressive left — treat every compromise as surrender.

senators.name: Marco Rubio
senators.blurb: The Tea Party's polished new star from Florida, elected over the establishment's pick: young, ambitious, and allergic to anything that smells like business as usual.

senators.name: Rand Paul
senators.blurb: The movement's ideologist: an eye doctor who reads deficits as theft and most federal programs as unconstitutional. Compromise is what he came to Washington to stop.

senators.name: Bernie Sanders
senators.blurb: Vermont's socialist, now a national voice against austerity — he recently held the floor for eight and a half hours against extending the Bush tax cuts.

senators.name: Scott Brown
senators.blurb: The Massachusetts surprise: a Republican in Ted Kennedy's old seat, driving a pickup truck and hugging the center line for dear life before 2012.

senators.name: Claire McCaskill
senators.blurb: A Missouri Democrat with an auditor's temperament, hunting earmarks and waste to survive in a state drifting steadily redder.

bills.name: The Debt-Ceiling Deal
bills.desc: August 2, 2011 — default is hours away. The Budget Control Act raises the debt ceiling in exchange for a decade of spending caps and an automatic 'sequester' guillotine. Nobody loves it, and leadership is begging for votes from the middle outward.
bills.question: On the Motion (Motion to Concur in the House Amendment to S. 365)

bills.name: Violence Against Women Act
bills.desc: April 26, 2012. Reauthorizing the Violence Against Women Act — routine for eighteen years, now a fight over newly added protections for Native American, immigrant, and LGBT victims.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill S. 1925

bills.name: The Buffett Rule
bills.desc: April 16, 2012. A minimum 30% tax rate on incomes over $1 million, named for the billionaire who pays a lower rate than his secretary. It needs 60 votes to advance and everyone knows it won't get them — this vote is for the campaign ads.
bills.question: On Cloture on the Motion to Proceed S. 2230
bills.result: Cloture on the Motion to Proceed Rejected

puzzles.title: Election Year Fever
puzzles.intro: An impeachment ends, a pandemic begins, and a Supreme Court seat opens six weeks before the election. 2020 asks every senator exactly one question: how brave are you feeling?

senators.name: Susan Collins
senators.blurb: The last New England Republican, defending a seat in a state Trump lost. Her approval has cratered since the Kavanaugh vote, and every roll call is scrutinized for daylight from the President.

senators.name: Mitt Romney
senators.blurb: The party's 2012 nominee, reborn as its most conspicuous internal critic — the first senator in history to vote to remove a president of his own party.

senators.name: Bernie Sanders
senators.blurb: Running for president again as the progressive standard-bearer, against the trade deals and foreign wars of both parties' establishments.

senators.name: Kyrsten Sinema
senators.blurb: Arizona's unpredictable first-termer: a former Green Party activist turned proud centrist who prizes her bipartisan brand above her party's agenda.

senators.name: Tom Cotton
senators.blurb: An Army veteran of Iraq and Afghanistan and the Senate's most disciplined hawk: maximum pressure abroad, maximum loyalty to the President at home.

bills.name: The USMCA Trade Deal
bills.desc: January 16, 2020. NAFTA's replacement: modernized rules, tougher labor enforcement in Mexico, and the rare blessing of both the AFL-CIO and the Chamber of Commerce — the last major bill before the impeachment trial consumes the chamber.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 5430

bills.name: Iran War Powers Resolution
bills.desc: February 13, 2020. Weeks after the drone strike killing Qassem Soleimani brought the U.S. and Iran to the brink, a resolution ordering the President to halt hostilities against Iran absent congressional authorization. A veto is certain; the vote is about the principle.
bills.question: On the Joint Resolution S.J.Res. 68

bills.name: Confirming Amy Coney Barrett
bills.desc: October 26, 2020 — eight days before the election, with millions of votes already cast. Amy Coney Barrett's confirmation to Ruth Bader Ginsburg's seat, four years after the majority held Scalia's seat open for ten months on election-year principle.
bills.question: On the Nomination PN2252

puzzles.title: The Fifty-Fifty Senate
puzzles.intro: A dead-even chamber where the Vice President breaks ties and any single senator holds veto power. Somehow, it becomes the most legislatively productive Congress in a generation.

senators.name: Mitch McConnell
senators.blurb: The Republican leader and self-described Grim Reaper of Democratic bills — but a master tactician who occasionally deals when he judges the party's long-term interest demands it.

senators.name: Lisa Murkowski
senators.blurb: Alaska's write-in survivor, openly estranged from Trump and facing his endorsed challenger next year — as independent as a Republican can be and still keep the label.

senators.name: Ted Cruz
senators.blurb: Still the Senate's happiest warrior of the right, with 2024 on his mind and a camera never far away.

senators.name: Tammy Baldwin
senators.blurb: Wisconsin's workmanlike progressive and the first openly gay senator — a patient vote-counter who prefers quietly assembling coalitions to cable-news combat.

senators.name: Kyrsten Sinema
senators.blurb: Now the Senate's most-watched Democrat alongside Joe Manchin: filibuster defender, dealmaker on infrastructure and guns, and scourge of her own party's left wing.

bills.name: The American Rescue Plan
bills.desc: March 6, 2021. $1.9 trillion in pandemic relief: $1,400 checks, an expanded child tax credit, and vaccine money, pushed through budget reconciliation after an all-night vote-a-rama. The new President wants it passed before unemployment benefits lapse.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 1319

bills.name: The Infrastructure Law
bills.desc: August 10, 2021. $550 billion in new money for roads, bridges, broadband, and transit, negotiated by ten senators from the middle. The question isn't whether it passes — it's which Republicans dare to touch it.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 3684

bills.name: The Respect for Marriage Act
bills.desc: November 29, 2022. Federal recognition of same-sex and interracial marriages, written as insurance after the Dobbs decision shook faith in precedent. Twenty-six years after DOMA passed this chamber 85–14, the Senate votes on marriage again.
bills.question: On Passage of the Bill H.R. 8404
